title: Maṅgalācaraṇa — The Closing Auspicious Verses (Maṅgalaṁ Bhagavān Vīro)
summary: This is a short garland of five auspicious verses recited at the very end of the Rāi (dawn) pratikramaṇa, the daily rite in which a practitioner reflects on and confesses the faults of the night. After the long work of confession is done, these verses send the practitioner back into the day on a note of blessing: they hail Lord Mahāvīra and other great souls, lovingly remember Gautamasvāmī as a giver of well-being, and end by proclaiming that the teaching of the Jinas is supreme and victorious. You do not need any technical background to follow them — they are meant to leave the heart light, hopeful, and auspiciously oriented.
ritualPlacement: These verses are the closing maṅgala of the Rātrika (Rāi) pratikramaṇa, recited just before the colophon. In the recitation tree they come after the Kevalnāṇī bṛhat-caityavandana and the Bharahesar sajjhāy (Rāi vidhi → Kevalnāṇī caityavandana → Bharahesar sajjhāy → these Maṅgalācaraṇa verses → colophon). Their function (kind: stuti — praise/benediction) is to seal the completed rite. They belong specifically to Rāi: Rāi shares the six core Āvaśyaka sūtras from the base Devasi pratikramaṇa but adds its own distinctive material — this caityavandana, the Bharahesar sajjhāy, and these closing maṅgala verses. Note that the word 'maṅgalācaraṇa' literally means an auspicious opening invocation; here the same auspicious-framing function is placed at the close, so do not confuse these Rāi closing verses with the Navkar/maṅgala that opens the Devasi pratikramaṇa. Unlike the shared Āvaśyaka sūtras, this is Rāi-specific text and is not carried into Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī by the usual word-substitution rule.
authorAndSource: Composite. Verses 1 and 5 are well-known Sanskrit maṅgala/praśasti ślokas of broad Śvetāmbara currency, not ascribed to a single named author in this book; verse 5 ('Sarvamaṅgalamāṅgalyaṁ … Jainaṁ jayati śāsanam') is the Jain praśasti recension of a widely reused 'sarvamaṅgalamāṅgalyam' verse, here closing with the Jaina śāsana. Verses 2–4 are popular vernacular (classical Gujarati / Apabhraṁśa-flavoured) dohās venerating Gautamasvāmī; verses 3 ('Aṅgūṭhe amṛta vase…') and 4 ('Gāmataṇo pesāraṇo…') circulate widely as traditional Gautamasvāmī stuti dohās of anonymous/traditional authorship. The book is the golden source for the exact readings; no individual author is named in it for these verses. ('—' for a single author.)
structureArc: The five verses move from formal Sanskrit benediction, through warm vernacular devotion to Gautamasvāmī, back to a ringing Sanskrit proclamation. (1) A Sanskrit śloka declaring four exemplars auspicious — Bhagavān Mahāvīra ('Vīra'), Gautama, Sthūlabhadra and others — and asking that the Jaina dharma itself be auspicious. (2) A vernacular dohā naming great souls to be remembered at dawn (Jambū, Nemi/Nemikumāra, Vajra/'Vayar', and Gautamasvāmī). (3) The famous dohā on Gautamasvāmī — 'nectar dwells in his thumb, he is a treasury of labdhis' — invoking him as bestower of every wished-for fruit. (4) A dohā promising that one who remembers Guru Gautama on setting out finds desired food, a prospering household, and abundance (lacchī/Lakṣmī). (5) The closing Sanskrit praśasti: the Jaina śāsana is the most auspicious of the auspicious, the cause of all welfare, foremost of all dharmas, and ever-victorious. So vv.1–2 open with collective benediction, vv.3–4 focus devotion on Gautamasvāmī, and v.5 universalizes back out to the triumph of the whole teaching.
recurringImagery: Two motifs thread the set. First, maṅgala (auspiciousness) frames the whole: it opens verse 1 fourfold ('maṅgalaṁ … maṅgalaṁ …') and returns, intensified, in verse 5's superlative 'sarvamaṅgalamāṅgalyaṁ' (the auspiciousness of all that is auspicious) — the garland begins and ends on the same blessing-note. Second, Gautamasvāmī as wish-fulfilling refuge binds the central verses: the labdhi (supernormal attainment) imagery of verse 3 — amṛta (nectar) in his thumb, his being a 'treasury of labdhis' — flows directly into verse 4's worldly boons (food, domestic welfare, prosperity), so that remembrance (smaraṇa) of Gautama is presented as the practical engine of both spiritual and material maṅgala. Read together, the human exemplars of vv.1–4 are the concrete faces of the abstract, victorious śāsana acclaimed in v.5.
keyThemes: Auspicious sealing (maṅgala) of the completed dawn rite
Veneration of Mahāvīra and the lineage of liberated/great souls
Devotion to Gautamasvāmī as giver of wished-for fruits
Labdhi (spiritual attainment) overflowing into worldly well-being
Triumph and supremacy of the Jaina śāsana
Smaraṇa — auspicious remembrance at the start of the day

glossary.definition: Auspiciousness, blessing; an utterance that wards off obstacles and orients an action toward a good end.
An auspicious invocation; conventionally an opening benediction, here serving as the closing benediction of the Rāi rite.
A verse of praise or hymn of homage; the genre (kind) of this sutra.
The teaching, order, and discipline of the Jinas — the living institution of the Jaina faith.
Mahāvīra, the 24th and last Tīrthaṅkara of the present era; here hailed as auspicious.
Gautamasvāmī (Indrabhūti Gautama), the chief gaṇadhara of Mahāvīra, famed for his labdhis and revered as a giver of blessings.
A principal disciple of a Tīrthaṅkara who organizes the teaching; Gautama was Mahāvīra's foremost gaṇadhara.
A supernormal power or attainment arising from spiritual practice; Gautamasvāmī is called a 'treasury of labdhis'.
A celebrated early ācārya renowned for mastery over the passions; named among the auspicious in verse 1.
Jambūsvāmī, traditionally the last person of this era to attain kevala-jñāna (omniscience); remembered in verse 2.
Vajrasvāmī ('Vayar'), an early ācārya celebrated for his vidyās and labdhis; remembered in verse 2.
Nectar of immortality; in verse 3 a metaphor for the life-giving grace residing in Gautamasvāmī.
Devotional remembrance or invocation of a holy being, held to bring auspicious results.
The right path / teaching / moral order; in verse 5 the Jaina dharma is called foremost of all.
A laudatory or eulogistic verse; verse 5 is a praśasti proclaiming the victory of the Jaina śāsana.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply receive these as a closing blessing — feel the warmth of remembering great teachers and let the verses end the morning rite on an auspicious, hopeful note, leaning on the glossary for the few proper names. An advanced practitioner should note the deliberate Sanskrit–vernacular–Sanskrit arc, the bracketing of the set by maṅgala (vv.1 and 5), and the Gautamasvāmī labdhi-devotion of vv.3–4 as the experiential bridge between personal exemplars and the universal śāsana.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative verbatim verse readings and the Rāi placement of the closing maṅgala verses (the golden source).

sources.title: Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Gujarati dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa terms (labdhi, maṅgala) used in the vernacular dohās.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit term definitions: maṅgala, śāsana, praśasti, amṛta, dharma.

notes: The book is authoritative for every mātrā, anusvāra and conjunct; readings here follow it verbatim and are not normalized. Verses 1 and 5 are Sanskrit; verses 2–4 are classical-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa-flavoured dohās — the mixed register is original, not an editorial blend. No individual author is named in the book for any of the five verses; the attributions of vv.3–4 to the broad traditional Gautamasvāmī-stuti corpus reflect their wide liturgical circulation rather than a fixed textual ascription, and are marked traditional/uncertain. In verse 2 the exact identity of each named exemplar (e.g. 'Nemkumar' as Nemi/Nemikumāra, 'Vayar' as Vajrasvāmī) follows the conventional reading; should the book's footnotes carry a pāṭhāntara (variant reading), that variant governs. Verse 5's first hemistich echoes a widely reused 'sarvamaṅgalamāṅgalyam' verse-frame found across Indian traditions; only the Jain-specific close ('sarvadharmāṇāṁ Jainaṁ jayati śāsanam') is distinctive here, and no single originating scripture is asserted.
